Can a Blog Really Make Tens of Thousands of Dollars a Month? Real Google AdSense Numbers, Explained

This post is about whether blogging actually makes money. I start with my own blog's three revenue sources (Google AdSense, Buy Me A Coffee, Portaly) at roughly an 8:1:1 split, then zoom in on Google AdSense. Covers CPM vs. CPC, how AdBlock kills impression revenue, my Google Analytics monthly active users, the AdSense performance report (page views, impressions, clicks, CPC, page RPM), how to calculate actual monthly earnings, and finally my own ad placement strategy and why I try to balance revenue against reader experience.
Receiving Google AdSense Wire-Transfer Payouts (Richart / Taiwan Example)

Five-minute walkthrough of setting up Google AdSense wire-transfer payouts. The bank used here is Richart (Taishin International Bank, Taiwan) — the specifics are Taiwan-flavored, but the general flow (find your foreign-currency account details, fill in AdSense's wire details form, wait for the $100 threshold, claim the incoming wire) applies to AdSense payouts in any country.
Why Writing Posts Can Earn Money — Google AdSense and Liker, Explained

Did you know writing blog posts can earn you money? And did you know Google AdSense isn't the only path? This post is the intro version of how it works — Google AdSense (click revenue vs. impression revenue), how AdBlock affects you, what it takes to build real income, plus Liker as a like-based crypto-tipping alternative.
(30) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Tricks: Financial Freedom
This one's mostly chit-chat — the title is tongue-in-cheek, but writing a blog can actually make a tiny bit of money.
How to Increase Your Google AdSense Revenue (Placement Tips)

A short notes post on the small things that move the needle on blog AdSense revenue — mostly about ad placement. Covers picking the right ad type (responsive Display ads), how many ads is too many, slots that are reliably visible (sidebar, article top, article bottom), and the two Google AdSense rules you should never break (no asking users to click, no clicking your own ads).
